speaker: BOTTOM
text: Are we all met?

speaker: QUINCE
text: Pat, pat. And here’s a marvels convenient

speaker: QUINCE
text: place for our rehearsal. This green plot shall be

speaker: QUINCE
text: our stage, this hawthorn brake our tiring-house,

speaker: QUINCE
text: and we will do it in action as we will do it before

speaker: QUINCE
text: the Duke.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Peter Quince?

speaker: QUINCE
text: What sayest thou, bully Bottom?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: There are things in this comedy of Pyramus

speaker: BOTTOM
text: and Thisbe that will never please. First, Pyramus

speaker: BOTTOM
text: must draw a sword to kill himself, which the ladies

speaker: BOTTOM
text: cannot abide. How answer you that?

speaker: SNOUT
text: By ’r lakin, a parlous fear.

speaker: STARVELING
text: I believe we must leave the killing out,

speaker: STARVELING
text: when all is done.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Not a whit! I have a device to make all well.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Write me a prologue, and let the prologue seem to

speaker: BOTTOM
text: say we will do no harm with our swords and that

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Pyramus is not killed indeed. And, for the more

speaker: BOTTOM
text: better assurance, tell them that I, Pyramus, am not

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Pyramus, but Bottom the weaver. This will put them

speaker: BOTTOM
text: out of fear.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Well, we will have such a prologue, and it shall

speaker: QUINCE
text: be written in eight and six.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: No, make it two more. Let it be written in

speaker: BOTTOM
text: eight and eight.

speaker: SNOUT
text: Will not the ladies be afeard of the lion?

speaker: STARVELING
text: I fear it, I promise you.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Masters, you ought to consider with yourself,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: to bring in (God shield us!) a lion among ladies is a

speaker: BOTTOM
text: most dreadful thing. For there is not a more fearful

speaker: BOTTOM
text: wildfowl than your lion living, and we ought to look

speaker: BOTTOM
text: to ’t.

speaker: SNOUT
text: Therefore another prologue must tell he is not

speaker: SNOUT
text: a lion.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Nay, you must name his name, and half his

speaker: BOTTOM
text: face must be seen through the lion’s neck, and he

speaker: BOTTOM
text: himself must speak through, saying thus, or to the

speaker: BOTTOM
text: same defect: “Ladies,” or “Fair ladies, I would

speaker: BOTTOM
text: wish you,” or “I would request you,” or “I would

speaker: BOTTOM
text: entreat you not to fear, not to tremble! My life for

speaker: BOTTOM
text: yours. If you think I come hither as a lion, it were

speaker: BOTTOM
text: pity of my life. No, I am no such thing. I am a man as

speaker: BOTTOM
text: other men are.” And there indeed let him name his

speaker: BOTTOM
text: name and tell them plainly he is Snug the joiner.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Well, it shall be so. But there is two hard

speaker: QUINCE
text: things: that is, to bring the moonlight into a chamber,

speaker: QUINCE
text: for you know Pyramus and Thisbe meet by

speaker: QUINCE
text: moonlight.

speaker: SNOUT
text: Doth the moon shine that night we play our

speaker: SNOUT
text: play?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: A calendar, a calendar! Look in the almanac.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Find out moonshine, find out moonshine.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Yes, it doth shine that night.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Why, then, may you leave a casement of the

speaker: BOTTOM
text: great chamber window, where we play, open, and

speaker: BOTTOM
text: the moon may shine in at the casement.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Ay, or else one must come in with a bush of

speaker: QUINCE
text: thorns and a lantern and say he comes to disfigure

speaker: QUINCE
text: or to present the person of Moonshine. Then there

speaker: QUINCE
text: is another thing: we must have a wall in the great

speaker: QUINCE
text: chamber, for Pyramus and Thisbe, says the story,

speaker: QUINCE
text: did talk through the chink of a wall.

speaker: SNOUT
text: You can never bring in a wall. What say you,

speaker: SNOUT
text: Bottom?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Some man or other must present Wall. And

speaker: BOTTOM
text: let him have some plaster, or some loam, or some

speaker: BOTTOM
text: roughcast about him to signify wall, or let him

speaker: BOTTOM
text: hold his fingers thus, and through that cranny shall

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Pyramus and Thisbe whisper.

speaker: QUINCE
text: If that may be, then all is well. Come, sit down,

speaker: QUINCE
text: every mother’s son, and rehearse your parts. Pyramus,

speaker: QUINCE
text: you begin. When you have spoken your

speaker: QUINCE
text: speech, enter into that brake, and so everyone

speaker: QUINCE
text: according to his cue.

speaker: ROBIN
text: What hempen homespuns have we swagg’ring here

speaker: ROBIN
text: So near the cradle of the Fairy Queen?

speaker: ROBIN
text: What, a play toward? I’ll be an auditor—

speaker: ROBIN
text: An actor too perhaps, if I see cause.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Speak, Pyramus.—Thisbe, stand forth.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Thisbe, the flowers of odious savors sweet—

speaker: QUINCE
text: Odors, odors!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: …odors savors sweet.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: So hath thy breath, my dearest Thisbe dear.—

speaker: BOTTOM
text: But hark, a voice! Stay thou but here awhile,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: And by and by I will to thee appear.

speaker: ROBIN
text: A stranger Pyramus than e’er played here.

speaker: FLUTE
text: Must I speak now?

speaker: QUINCE
text: Ay, marry, must you, for you must understand

speaker: QUINCE
text: he goes but to see a noise that he heard and is to

speaker: QUINCE
text: come again.

speaker: FLUTE
text: Most radiant Pyramus, most lily-white of hue,

speaker: FLUTE
text: Of color like the red rose on triumphant brier,

speaker: FLUTE
text: Most brisky juvenal and eke most lovely Jew,

speaker: FLUTE
text: As true as truest horse, that yet would never tire.

speaker: FLUTE
text: I’ll meet thee, Pyramus, at Ninny’s tomb.

speaker: QUINCE
text: “Ninus’ tomb,” man! Why, you must not

speaker: QUINCE
text: speak that yet. That you answer to Pyramus. You

speaker: QUINCE
text: speak all your part at once, cues and all.—Pyramus,

speaker: QUINCE
text: enter. Your cue is past. It is “never tire.”

speaker: FLUTE
text: O!

speaker: FLUTE
text:
As
                 
                Thisbe
                .
                 
              As true as truest horse, that yet would never tire.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: If I were fair, fair Thisbe, I were only thine.

speaker: QUINCE
text: O monstrous! O strange! We are haunted. Pray,

speaker: QUINCE
text: masters, fly, masters! Help!

speaker: ROBIN
text: I’ll follow you. I’ll lead you about a round,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Through bog, through bush, through brake, through brier.

speaker: ROBIN
text: Sometime a horse I’ll be, sometime a hound,

speaker: ROBIN
text: A hog, a headless bear, sometime a fire,

speaker: ROBIN
text: And neigh and bark and grunt and roar and burn,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Like horse, hound, hog, bear, fire, at every turn.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Why do they run away? This is a knavery of

speaker: BOTTOM
text: them to make me afeard.

speaker: SNOUT
text: O Bottom, thou art changed! What do I see on

speaker: SNOUT
text: thee?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: What do you see? You see an ass-head of your

speaker: BOTTOM
text: own, do you?

speaker: QUINCE
text: Bless thee, Bottom, bless thee! Thou art

speaker: QUINCE
text: translated!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I see their knavery. This is to make an ass of

speaker: BOTTOM
text: me, to fright me, if they could. But I will not stir

speaker: BOTTOM
text: from this place, do what they can. I will walk up

speaker: BOTTOM
text: and down here, and I will sing, that they shall hear

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I am not afraid.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The ouzel cock, so black of hue,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: With orange-tawny bill,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The throstle with his note so true,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The wren with little quill—

speaker: TITANIA
text: What angel wakes me from my flow’ry bed?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The finch, the sparrow, and the lark,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The plainsong cuckoo gray,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Whose note full many a man doth mark

speaker: BOTTOM
text: And dares not answer “nay”—

speaker: BOTTOM
text: for, indeed, who would set his wit to so foolish a

speaker: BOTTOM
text: bird? Who would give a bird the lie though he cry

speaker: BOTTOM
text: “cuckoo” never so?

speaker: TITANIA
text: I pray thee, gentle mortal, sing again.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Mine ear is much enamored of thy note,

speaker: TITANIA
text: So is mine eye enthrallèd to thy shape,

speaker: TITANIA
text: And thy fair virtue’s force perforce doth move me

speaker: TITANIA
text: On the first view to say, to swear, I love thee.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Methinks, mistress, you should have little

speaker: BOTTOM
text: reason for that. And yet, to say the truth, reason

speaker: BOTTOM
text: and love keep little company together nowadays.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The more the pity that some honest neighbors will

speaker: BOTTOM
text: not make them friends. Nay, I can gleek upon

speaker: BOTTOM
text: occasion.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Thou art as wise as thou art beautiful.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Not so neither; but if I had wit enough to get

speaker: BOTTOM
text: out of this wood, I have enough to serve mine own

speaker: BOTTOM
text: turn.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Out of this wood do not desire to go.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Thou shalt remain here whether thou wilt or no.

speaker: TITANIA
text: I am a spirit of no common rate.

speaker: TITANIA
text: The summer still doth tend upon my state,

speaker: TITANIA
text: And I do love thee. Therefore go with me.

speaker: TITANIA
text: I’ll give thee fairies to attend on thee,

speaker: TITANIA
text: And they shall fetch thee jewels from the deep

speaker: TITANIA
text: And sing while thou on pressèd flowers dost sleep.

speaker: TITANIA
text: And I will purge thy mortal grossness so

speaker: TITANIA
text: That thou shalt like an airy spirit go.—

speaker: TITANIA
text: Peaseblossom, Cobweb, Mote, and Mustardseed!

speaker: PEASEBLOSSOM
text: Ready.

speaker: COBWEB
text: And I.

speaker: MOTE
text: And I.

speaker: MUSTARDSEED
text: And I.

speaker: ALL
text: Where shall we go?

speaker: TITANIA
text: Be kind and courteous to this gentleman.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Hop in his walks and gambol in his eyes;

speaker: TITANIA
text: Feed him with apricocks and dewberries,

speaker: TITANIA
text: With purple grapes, green figs, and mulberries;

speaker: TITANIA
text: The honey-bags steal from the humble-bees,

speaker: TITANIA
text: And for night-tapers crop their waxen thighs

speaker: TITANIA
text: And light them at the fiery glowworms’ eyes

speaker: TITANIA
text: To have my love to bed and to arise;

speaker: TITANIA
text: And pluck the wings from painted butterflies

speaker: TITANIA
text: To fan the moonbeams from his sleeping eyes.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Nod to him, elves, and do him courtesies.

speaker: PEASEBLOSSOM
text: Hail, mortal!

speaker: COBWEB
text: Hail!

speaker: MOTE
text: Hail!

speaker: MUSTARDSEED
text: Hail!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I cry your Worships mercy, heartily.—I beseech

speaker: BOTTOM
text: your Worship’s name.

speaker: COBWEB
text: Cobweb.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I shall desire you of more acquaintance, good

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Master Cobweb. If I cut my finger, I shall make

speaker: BOTTOM
text: bold with you.—Your name, honest gentleman?

speaker: PEASEBLOSSOM
text: Peaseblossom.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I pray you, commend me to Mistress Squash,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: your mother, and to Master Peascod, your father.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Good Master Peaseblossom, I shall desire you of

speaker: BOTTOM
text: more acquaintance too.—Your name, I beseech

speaker: BOTTOM
text: you, sir?

speaker: MUSTARDSEED
text: Mustardseed.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Good Master Mustardseed, I know your patience

speaker: BOTTOM
text: well. That same cowardly, giantlike ox-beef

speaker: BOTTOM
text: hath devoured many a gentleman of your house. I

speaker: BOTTOM
text: promise you, your kindred hath made my eyes

speaker: BOTTOM
text: water ere now. I desire you of more acquaintance,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: good Master Mustardseed.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Come, wait upon him. Lead him to my bower.

speaker: TITANIA
text: The moon, methinks, looks with a wat’ry eye,

speaker: TITANIA
text: And when she weeps, weeps every little flower,

speaker: TITANIA
text: Lamenting some enforcèd chastity.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Tie up my lover’s tongue. Bring him silently.
